other_side: NLW's lead is that the American economy now is the AI trade, that the labs need ceaseless token-consumption growth, that enterprises are slamming on spending caps, and that only mass-scale AI training can square the two by lifting people from assisted to agentic AI. Writing the day before, Zitron reads the identical facts as terminal: token-based billing has triggered "a revolt in the AI industry's customer base," because what the tokens cost is far higher than the value they create. He treats the very "train them to use more" move NLW predicts as proof of desperation — labs manufacturing token burn to feed a circular economy with no honest demand beneath it. Where NLW sees training as the bridge to value, Zitron sees an unprofitable product whose training costs alone drown out every dollar of revenue.
truth_beneath: Both men orbit one variable: whether enterprise spending caps reflect a fixable adoption gap or a permanent gap between AI's cost and its value. They accept the same facts — the AI trade now drives much of US growth, Uber, Walmart and Meta have capped or restructured token spend, and the labs will push enablement, training and agent "loops" to lift usage. The empirical question that resolves is whether, after training, capped enterprises raise their budgets and show output worth the spend; the observable is token budgets rising post-enablement with attributable revenue, versus caps tightening regardless. The residue is worldview: does training create value or merely engineer demand? NLW is vindicated if enablement lifts caps and value follows; Zitron if training is pure demand-manufacture and ROI stays unmeasurable. Both are polemicists with incentives — Zitron anti-hype and bubble-certain; NLW anti-doom and, tellingly, himself selling training — yet both independently centre the same caps and the same "make people burn more tokens" turn. Watch next quarter's enterprise token budgets.
